title: Cat Excessive Scratching at Ear Base: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat scratching their ear base? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and how to help your vet with triage photos.
content_html:
What is it?

Excessive scratching at the ear base is a common sign of feline ear discomfort. When a cat paws at the area behind or under the ear flap, it usually indicates inflammation or irritation within the ear canal or on the surrounding skin. This behavior is often triggered by common issues such as ear mites, bacterial or yeast infections, environmental allergies, or foreign bodies lodged in the ear.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. In most cases, ear scratching is not a life-threatening emergency. However, while the urgency is low, the discomfort level for your cat is often quite high. We recommend scheduling a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. If left untreated, chronic scratching can cause the ear flap to swell with blood (aural hematoma) or lead to painful secondary skin infections.

Why a photo helps triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of the ear base and the inside of the ear flap is incredibly helpful for our triage team. Visualizing the color of the skin, the presence of any discharge (such as dark, waxy, or pus-like material), and any visible sores allows us to better assess the severity of the irritation and provide more specific advice until you can reach the clinic.

faqs.question: What does dark, crumbly ear debris mean?
faqs.answer: Dark debris that resembles coffee grounds is a classic sign of ear mites, a common parasite in cats that requires specific anti-parasitic treatment.

faqs.question: Can I clean my cat's ears at home before the vet visit?
faqs.answer: It is generally best to avoid cleaning the ears right before your appointment, as the veterinarian needs to sample the discharge to identify the cause of the infection.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to ear issues?
faqs.answer: While any cat can develop ear problems, cats with allergies or those with smaller ear canals may be more susceptible to recurrent infections.
